pattern: A value's occurrences only need to be listed once, in order, at construction time; after that, counting how many fall inside a range is just finding two boundaries in that already-sorted list.
hints: Group the array's indices by the value stored there, once, when the structure is built -- each group comes out already sorted since you scan left to right.
A query only cares about one value's group; if that value never appeared, the answer is immediately zero.

Within that value's sorted index list, binary search for the first index not less than the query's left bound and the first index past the query's right bound -- the gap between those two positions is the count.

edge_inputs.description: several queries against one small array, mixing a full scan and a tight window
a value that never appears anywhere in the array
single-cell subarray queries where left equals right
the same value once clustered at the start and once scattered later, queried separately
a query spanning the entire array
back-to-back queries reuse the same value across disjoint and overlapping ranges
a single-element array queried for a present and an absent value

stress.description: a moderately large random array followed by many random range queries mixing present and absent values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 2000)
    arr = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    ops = ['RangeFreqQuery']
    args = [[arr]]
    num_queries = min(max(1, size), 400)
    for _ in range(num_queries):
        left = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        right = rng.randint(left, n - 1)
        if rng.random() < 0.2:
            value = rng.randint(1, 10000)
        else:
            value = arr[rng.randint(0, n - 1)]
        ops.append('query')
        args.append([left, right, value])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: about one in five queries asks for a value that may not be present, forcing the empty-group path

complexity.time: O(n) build, O(log n) per query
